title: Getting Acquainted
intro:
This lesson introduces repetitive, socially det.mp3ined items used for interaction with f.mp3al acquaintances. A selected set of honorific items is introduced with such other honorific exponents such as the plural f.mp3s of pronouns and verbs. All the participants are Hindus. Therefore, one finds the use of echo-greetings, such as namaska:r. Note the following recurring greeting-denoting collocations:

Prabha: Greetings, Sheelaji. How are you? Sheela: Greetings, I am fine. How are you? In Kashmiri society, if two persons are engaged in conversation and a third one appears who is known only to one of them, he is not necessarily f.mp3ally introduced to the other person. He just watches, listens, or smiles uncomfortably. However, this is slowly changing. Therefore, we have introduced the following: Prabha: I am fine. Do you know each other? She is my friend. Sheela: Greetings. What is your name? A conversation between, and

exchanges.speaker: Prabha
exchanges.english: Greetings, Sheelaji. How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Sheela
exchanges.english: Greetings. I am fine. How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Prabha
exchanges.english: I am fine. Do you know each other? She is my friend.

exchanges.speaker: Sheela
exchanges.english: Greetings. What is your name?

exchanges.speaker: Dulari
exchanges.english: Greetings. My name is Dulari.

exchanges.speaker: Sheela
exchanges.english: Where do you live?

exchanges.speaker: Dulari
exchanges.english: We live in Delhi.

exchanges.speaker: Sheela
exchanges.english: When did you come to Kashmir?

exchanges.speaker: Dulari
exchanges.english: We came yesterday.

exchanges.speaker: Sheela
exchanges.english: How long will you stay in Kashmir?

exchanges.speaker: Dulari
exchanges.english: We shall stay for a m onth or two.

exchanges.speaker: Sheela
exchanges.english: Our home is nearby. Come, let us have (some) tea.

exchanges.speaker: Prabha
exchanges.english: No, not today. Some other time.

exchanges.speaker: Sheela
exchanges.english: Dulariji, you should come too.

exchanges.speaker: Dulari
exchanges.english: Yes, I will certainly come with Prabhaji.

exchanges.speaker: Sheela
exchanges.english: All right. Goodbye.

exchanges.speaker: Dulari and Sheela
exchanges.english: Goodbye.

title: A conversation with a coolie
intro: A conversation between a mozu:r 'laborer, coolie' and a tourist. The fixed collocations which may be used in bargaining for a price from shawls to sheep are: Ashok: Yes, but how much will you charge? Once a price is quoted by the seller, one habitually responds, Ashok: No, that is too much. In Kashmir, as in other parts of India, a mozu:r is ubiquitous and carries anything from firewood to fruit.

exchanges.speaker: Porter
exchanges.english: Do you have any luggage, sir?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, two cases and one bed-roll.

exchanges.speaker: Porter
exchanges.english: Shall I carry the luggage?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, but how much will you charge?

exchanges.speaker: Porter
exchanges.english: Where do you have to go? (Where are you going?)

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I have to go to Dal Gate.

exchanges.speaker: Porter
exchanges.english: Only ten rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, that is too much.

exchanges.speaker: Porter
exchanges.english: No, sir, this luggage is heavy. It will require two coolies.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Can we get another coolie?

exchanges.speaker: Porter
exchanges.english: Yes, sir, we are two.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, say how much you will charge.

exchanges.speaker: Porter
exchanges.english: Just ten rupees, sir.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, eight rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Porter
exchanges.english: All right, sir, let us go.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: This is (my) luggage.

title: A conversation with a hotel keeper
intro: In this conversation, a personal inquiry is made about the availability of accomodations, without previous reservations, in a hotel in Srinagar. The desk clerk is a Muslim, therefore, the mode of greeting is

exchanges.speaker: Hotel Keeper
exchanges.english: Greetings, sir.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, do you have a vacant room?

exchanges.speaker: Hotel Keeper
exchanges.english: Yes, sir, I do.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I want a room.

exchanges.speaker: Hotel Keeper
exchanges.english: How many days will you stay?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Two days.

exchanges.speaker: Hotel Keeper
exchanges.english: All right, please write your name on this register.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the charge?

exchanges.speaker: Hotel Keeper
exchanges.english: Do you want only the room, or room and board?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, just the room.

exchanges.speaker: Hotel Keeper
exchanges.english: The room is twenty rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, that's fine.

exchanges.speaker: Hotel Keeper
exchanges.english: Here is the key.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the room number?

exchanges.speaker: Hotel Keeper
exchanges.english: It is number four. Where is your luggage?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: The luggage is in taxi.

exchanges.speaker: Hotel Keeper
exchanges.english: You go to your room. The porter will bring in the luggage.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Fine, thank you.

title: A conversation with a houseboat owner
intro: The participants are a tourist, Ashok and, a houseboat owner. A houseboat is generally owned and managed by a single person, and is leased for lengths of time varying from an overnight stay to a full summer season's stay. On leasing a houseboat (often the lease is verbal), the set contextual phrases are as follows: Houseboat owner: Certainly, take a look at it. It's very beautiful. How many persons are there? Houseboat owner: For how long do you want to stay? (and, the invariable inquiry) Ashok: What is the charge? It is after this last inquiry that the unavoidable bargaining begins.

exchanges.speaker: Houseboat owner
exchanges.english: Greetings, Sahib. Come, Sahib, stay in the houseboat.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Well, I would like to see the houseboat.

exchanges.speaker: Houseboat owner
exchanges.english: Certainly, take a look at it. It's very beautiful. How many persons are there?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I am the only one.

exchanges.speaker: Houseboat owner
exchanges.english: For how long do you want to stay?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: One week.

exchanges.speaker: Houseboat owner
exchanges.english: All right, let's go.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How far is the houseboat?

exchanges.speaker: Houseboat owner
exchanges.english: Near Dal Gate.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How many rooms does it have?

exchanges.speaker: Houseboat owner
exchanges.english: Four rooms, two for sleeping (two bedrooms), one for sitting (one living room), and one for cooking and dining (and a dinette-kitchen).

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the charge?

exchanges.speaker: Houseboat owner
exchanges.english: Just twenty rupees a day.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, I will look at the houseboat.

exchanges.speaker: Houseboat owner
exchanges.english: Yes, sir, let's go.

title: A conversation with a tonga driver
intro: A conversation on hiring a light, horse-driven, two-wheeled carriage. This type of carriage is used all over the valley aaand is the main mode of transportation in the downtown areas. It can carry four persons and some luggage, too.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Hello, tongawala, will you go to Nehru Park?

exchanges.speaker: Tonga driver
exchanges.english: Yes, sir, climb in!

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How far is Nehru Park from here?

exchanges.speaker: Tonga driver
exchanges.english: It will take an hour (to get there).

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the charge?

exchanges.speaker: Tonga driver
exchanges.english: Ten rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I won't pay more than five rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Tonga driver
exchanges.english: No, that's not enough. Give me eight rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, let's go!

exchanges.speaker: Tonga driver
exchanges.english: All right. Let me feed the horse (some) hay, and (then) we'll leave.

title: A conversation with a boatman
intro: A conversation on hiring a shikara, a particular type of boat used for sightseeing. The shikaras are specially decorated and have cushions with springs. There are several types of shikaras used for various purposes. This conversation pertains to a sightseeing trip on Dal Lake. A prolonged (and enjoyable) discussion about the 'rate' invariably f.mp3s a part of the hiring process. Note particularly the response to the question: What is the rate from here? Then note the boatman's response: Whatever you like, sir. This is a frequent response to inquiries about prices, rates, etc. On the whole, prices are negotiable, and the response does not necessarily mean that the buyer has a choice. All this indicates is that the seller is open to bargaining.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is this your boat?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: Yes, sir, what can I do for you?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Will you go to Nishat (Garden)?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: Yes, come on down (here).

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How long will it take? Boatman: One hour. Ashok: What is the rate from here?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: Whatever you like, sir.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, (tell me) what is the rate?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: Eight rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, six rupees is a fair price.

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: All right, come on down.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, let's go.

title: A conversation with a taxi driver
intro: The traditional mode of transportation in Kashmir has not always been a taxi. It has only recently been introduced in Srinagar, and is fast becoming popular. It is now replacing the fascinating horse-drawn carriage (tonga) in certain areas.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is the taxi available (for hire)?

exchanges.speaker: Taxi driver
exchanges.english: Yes, (please) get in. Where do you want to go?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I want to go to the post office.

exchanges.speaker: Taxi driver
exchanges.english: Which post office?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: The head post office.

exchanges.speaker: Taxi driver
exchanges.english: All right, come on in.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How far is it?

exchanges.speaker: Taxi driver
exchanges.english: It is two miles from here.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, (please) hurry.

exchanges.speaker: Taxi driver
exchanges.english: All right, sir, (we're) on our way.

title: A conversation with a tea seller
intro: There are no tea-stalls in Kashmir other than very temporary set-ups at festivals and carnivals, or on Sundays or holidays at the Mughal gardens. A cup (or a pot) of tea may, however, be ordered in several places which sell snacks and/or sweetmeats.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Give me a cup of tea, please.

exchanges.speaker: Tea seller
exchanges.english: Please sit down. What kind of tea, Kashmiri or Lipton?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Lipton.

exchanges.speaker: Tea seller
exchanges.english: Shall I add sugar and milk?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, but only a little.

exchanges.speaker: Tea seller
exchanges.english: Here you are.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much do I pay?

exchanges.speaker: Tea seller
exchanges.english: Just fifty paise.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Here, take this one-rupee bill (note).

exchanges.speaker: Tea seller
exchanges.english: Here is your change.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Thank you.

title: A conversation at the post office
intro: This concerns a conversation at the post office between a postal clerk and a tourist (Ashok) about buying various types of postage items such as, stamps, inland a.mp3ail letters, aerograms, etc. A serious task such as mailing registered letters or packages is a test of endurance and patience. But, by and large, things get done and the mail keeps moving.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: (Please) give me stamps for ten rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Clerk
exchanges.english: What denomination, sir?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Three for the United States, two for Delhi, and could you also give me four aerograms?

exchanges.speaker: Clerk
exchanges.english: Here are the stamps and the aerograms, and here is (your) change.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Thank you. This letter is to be sent by registered mail.

exchanges.speaker: Clerk
exchanges.english: A.mp3ail, or (would you prefer) surface mail?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, a.mp3ail.

exchanges.speaker: Clerk
exchanges.english: All right, give it to me. I shall weigh it.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Take it, please.

exchanges.speaker: Clerk
exchanges.english: It will cost four rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, here is a five rupee bill.

exchanges.speaker: Clerk
exchanges.english: Here is your receipt, and here is the change.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, thank you.

title: A conversation with a milkman
intro: This concerns a conversation with a traditional 'milkman', about arranging home delivery of milk or yogurt. The t.mp3 gu:r also means 'milkman'. The use of bottled milk is still restricted to a few areas in Srinagar and some nearby towns. A brief discussion on prices etc. is also included.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much is a kilo of milk?

exchanges.speaker: Milkman
exchanges.english: Two rupees, sir.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Milk or yogurt?

exchanges.speaker: Milkman
exchanges.english: Yes, milk and yogurt both sell for two rupees a kilo.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What price is the cheese?

exchanges.speaker: Milkman
exchanges.english: Five rupees a kilo.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Do you have home delivery of milk?

exchanges.speaker: Milkman
exchanges.english: Yes, every morning. Where do you live?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: In the white house, right here near the bend.

exchanges.speaker: Milkman
exchanges.english: We'll deliver it for you. How much do you want?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: One kilo of milk and one kilo of yogurt.

exchanges.speaker: Milkman
exchanges.english: Very well, sir.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Do you want a deposit?

exchanges.speaker: Milkman
exchanges.english: No, we shall keep a monthly account and send you the bill each month.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Fine, thank you.

title: A conversation with a fruit seller
intro: A conversation between a tourist (Ashok) and a Kashmiri fruit seller. A few Kashmiri fruits, such as 'apples', 'cherries', and 'stunted apples' are mentioned. On every street corner in Kashmir, there are fruit sellers selling the fruits that are in season. They have very interesting 'peddlar's cries' which they use to attract customers.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is this fruit?

exchanges.speaker: Fruit seller
exchanges.english: These are stunted apples.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Fine! Are these cherries?

title: A conversation with a vegetable seller
intro: A conversation about buying vegetables from a traditional vegetable seller. The names of popular Kashmir vegetables are introduced.

exchanges.speaker: Vegetable seller
exchanges.english: What would you like, sir?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Are there any fresh vegetables?

exchanges.speaker: Vegetable seller
exchanges.english: Everything is fresh. The spinach and potatoes have just arrived. The radishes, eggplants, and cucumbers are also fresh.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the price of spinach and potatoes?

exchanges.speaker: Vegetable seller
exchanges.english: Spinach is one rupee a kilo. Potatoes are one rupee and fifty paise a kilo.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much are radishes and eggplants?

exchanges.speaker: Vegetable seller
exchanges.english: A bunch of radishes is ten paise, and each eggplant is six paise.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Are cucumbers also sold by the kilo?

exchanges.speaker: Vegetable seller
exchanges.english: No, sir, cucumbers are twenty paise each.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, give me one kilo of potatoes, two kilos of spinach, and four bunches of radishes.

exchanges.speaker: Vegetable seller
exchanges.english: All right, (will there be) anything else?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, here is your money.

exchanges.speaker: Vegetable seller
exchanges.english: Thank you.

title: A conversation with a sweetmeat seller
intro: A conversation about buying Indian sweetmeats from a sweetmeat seller. There are no typically Kashmiri sweetmeats, hence all the names are non-Kashmiri.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What kind of sweetmeats are on this plate?

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: These are.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much do these cost?

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: They are six rupees a kilo.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I see. Do you sell these by the kilo?

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: Yes, are sold by the kilo.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is on these plates?

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: This is coconut. This is. These are and these are.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What has been pasted on the top of these sweetmeats?

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: These are silver leaves and gold leaves.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Does one eat them?

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: Yes, of course.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Did you sell all these sweets by the kilo?

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: No, we sell and by the dozen.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much are they per dozen?

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: Both are four rupees a dozen.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much are and?

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: Eight rupees a kilo.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Give me half a kilo of gulab and half a kilo of.

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: Anything else?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Well yes, also give me six and six.

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: Here they are.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much is it?

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: The total is seven rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Here take this ten rupees bill.

exchanges.speaker: Sweetmeat seller
exchanges.english: This is your change.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Thank you.

title: A conversation with a flower seller
intro: A conversation about buying flowers from a woman flower seller. These are sold fresh from shikaras, which go from houseboat to houseboat. There are also a few shops which sell flowers. In the city, a vegetable seller keeps seasonal flowers, such as lotuses and marigolds, which are used by the Kashmiri Pandits for the morning prayers.

exchanges.speaker: Flower seller
exchanges.english: Would you like to buy (some) flowers, sir?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, what kind of flowers do you have?

exchanges.speaker: Flower seller
exchanges.english: (Here) you see (I have) roses, lotuses, and narcissi.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Are these flowers fresh?

exchanges.speaker: Flower seller
exchanges.english: Yes, sir, these are absolutely fresh flowers. You may see (for yourself).

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right. How much are roses?

exchanges.speaker: Flower seller
exchanges.english: The roses are two rupees a bouquet.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What price are lotuses and narcissi?

exchanges.speaker: Flower seller
exchanges.english: A bouquet of lotuses or narcissi is one rupee.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, give me (some) roses and (some) lotuses.

exchanges.speaker: Flower seller
exchanges.english: (Do you want) red or yello roses?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: (Not yellow) red ones.

exchanges.speaker: Flower seller
exchanges.english: Here, please take them.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much money will that be?

exchanges.speaker: Flower seller
exchanges.english: Three rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I have a five rupee bill.

exchanges.speaker: Flower seller
exchanges.english: That is all right. I have change.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, (I hope you will) come again tomorrow.

title: A conversation with a grocer
intro: A conversation on buying groceries from a traditional grocer. The names of commonly used Kashmiri spices are introduced.

exchanges.speaker: Grocer
exchanges.english: What would you like, sir?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I have a list of things.

exchanges.speaker: Grocer
exchanges.english: Tell me the items. I shall note them down.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: One kilo of sugar, half a kilo of tea, three kilos of rice, and one kilo of da:l.

exchanges.speaker: Grocer
exchanges.english: Which type of da:l?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Beans.

exchanges.speaker: Grocer
exchanges.english: Would you like anything else?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, spices.

exchanges.speaker: Grocer
exchanges.english: What kind of spices?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Half a kilo each of t.mp3eric, ginger, fennel seed, and red pepper.

exchanges.speaker: Grocer
exchanges.english: Would you perhaps want some cardamom, etc.?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, one rupee's worth of cardamom and one rupee's worth of cinnamon. Oh yes, also one kilo of salt.

exchanges.speaker: Grocer
exchanges.english: Anything else?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, that is all. How long will it take?

exchanges.speaker: Grocer
exchanges.english: Just ten or fifteen minutes.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much is it?

exchanges.speaker: Grocer
exchanges.english: The total is thirty rupees and fifty paise.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Here is your money.

exchanges.speaker: Grocer
exchanges.english: Thank you.

title: A conversation with a butcher
intro: A conversation with a Kashmiri butcher about buying various types of meat. In Kashmir, meat generally refers to mutton.

exchanges.speaker: Butcher
exchanges.english: What would you like, sir?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I want one kilo of meat. What is the price?

exchanges.speaker: Butcher
exchanges.english: (Do you want it) with intestines or without intestines?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: (No) Without intestines.

exchanges.speaker: Butcher
exchanges.english: It is six rupees a kilo without intestines.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, give me one kilo.

exchanges.speaker: Butcher
exchanges.english: What else, sir?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I also want one kilo of ground meat and half a kilo of liver.

exchanges.speaker: Butcher
exchanges.english: All right, I will prepare the ground meat.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Are liver and ground meat both six rupees a kilo?

exchanges.speaker: Butcher
exchanges.english: No, sir, liver is six rupees a kilo, but the ground meat is eight rupees a kilo.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I see, the ground meat is more expensive than liver!

exchanges.speaker: Butcher
exchanges.english: But this is good quality ground meat.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the total bill?

exchanges.speaker: Butcher
exchanges.english: The total bill is seventeen rupees. How will you carry this meat?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Wrap it up (in paper). I will take it in this pot.

exchanges.speaker: Butcher
exchanges.english: All right.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Here is your money.

exchanges.speaker: Butcher
exchanges.english: Thank you.

title: A conversation on Kashmir
intro: An inf.mp3al conversation about the main physical features of Kashmir - its population, area, language, etc.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Greetings, Ashok.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, Mohan.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I am all right.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: When did you come to Kashmir?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I came yesterday. Kashmir is very beautiful.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, Kashmir is beautiful.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Do you live in Kashmir?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, I do (live in Kashmir).

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the population of Kashmir?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: The population of Kashmir is approximately forty-six lakhs.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How big is Kashmir?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: The total area of Kashmir is approximately two and a quarter lakh square kilometers.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is only Kashmiri spoken in Kashmir?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, In Kashmir only Kashmiri is spoken. But in Jammu, Dogri is spoken, and in Ladakh, Ladakhi is spoken.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Do you speak only Kashmiri?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, I speak only Kashmiri.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: There are other things concerning Kashmir that I want to talk to you about. But not today, I am in a hurry.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, certainly. Goodbye.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Goodbye.

title: The people of Kashmir
intro: A brief conversation about the religions, festivals, and the dress of Kashmiris. The main religious groups in Kashmir are Muslim, Hindu, Sikh, and Buddhist. There is a brief discussion on the and saree. The is a g.mp3ent worn in Kashmir.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, Mohanji.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Greetings, Ashok. How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I am fine. I am reading a book on Kashmir.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Really? That is good.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: It is not a particularly good book. That is why I have to ask you some questions concerning Kashmir.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, certainly.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the religion of Kashmiris?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Kashmiris are not of one religion. We have Muslims, Hindus, Sikhs and Buddhists.

title: Kashmiri food
intro: A conversation about various vegetarian and non-vegetarian Kashmiri dishes.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, Mohan. How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Greetings, Ashok. How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Today I have (some) questions about Kashmiri food.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, certainly.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the (typical) food of Kashmiris?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: The Kashmiris eat rice both in the morning and in the evening. All the people in Jammu eat bread (chapatis).

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Does paddy grow in Kashmir?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, of course.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Do people in Kashmir eat meat?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, in Kashmir, both Muslims and Hindus eat meat.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Tell me, is meat expensive?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, meat is more expensive than vegetables.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Which vegetables of Kashmir are well-known.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Kashmiris eat many kinds of vegetables, but the famous ones are, lotus stem, and eggplant.

title: On sight-seeing in Kashmir
intro: A conversation on the places worth visiting in and around Srinagar. The main tourist attractions mentioned are Gulmarg, Pahalgam and Wular Lake.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, Mohan.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Greetings, what's new?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I am thinking of going sight-seeing outside Srinagar. (Please) tell me how I should go about it.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Do you like horseback riding, boating, or hiking?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I like both horseback riding and boating.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: How many weeks do you want to spend sight-seeing?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: On or two weeks.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Very well, for horseback riding you go to Gulmarg and Pahalgam. Wular Lake is the best place for boating.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How far is Wular Lake?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: It should be about thirty miles from Srinagar. But from Wular Lake you can also go to Bandipur and Gurez.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Where does one get the bus or taxi for sight-seeing?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: You can make all the (necessary) arrangements at the Tourist Reception Center or at Amira Kadal.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Does one also get literature (books) about these places at the Tourist Center?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, of course, everything.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right. Sorry, I have to go now. Goodbye.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Goodbye.

title: Going on a boat ride
intro: This covers a conversation about the places in Kashmir which can be visited in a shikara. There is also a brief discussion on a large boat, and bahats (a barge).

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, Mohan.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: How are things?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Fine! I came today to talk with you about sight-seeing in a boat. I must go sight-seeing in a boat.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Well, that is not difficult. In Srinagar we have Dal Lake and the Vitasta River (Jehlum). Ashok: Yes, I know that.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: There are shikaras and houseboats in Dal Lake. There are shikaras, barges, and "a large boat" on the Jehlum River.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is a bahats?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: A bahats is a very big boat in whichfirewood, paddy, and other loads are carried. Also, people live on it.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is in Dal Lake?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: There are beautiful shikaras on Dal Lake. You can take a boat from the Dal Gate and then go to Nigin Lake and to the flower gardens. Two to four hours are enough. Ashok: How much will it cost?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Just five or six rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Five or six rupees is not very much.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: But sight-seeing on a boat on Vitasta River is expensive.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Why?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: It is a long distance from Amira Kadal (bridge) to the Weir. However, you will see the whole city. It will cost you just ten to twelve rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How long will it take?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Just four or five hours.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Where else is sight-seeing by boat possible?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: You (can) go from Srinagar to Wular Lake. It will take you three or four nights.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is Wular Lake a big lake?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, it is the second largest lake in Asia.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How big is Wular Lake?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: It is spread over twelve and a half square miles.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Well, I must go sight-seeing by a boat in Kashmir.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, of course.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Thank you. I must go now.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: All right, goodbye.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Goodbye.

title: A Kashmiri fire-pot
intro: This covers a conversation with a duka:nda:r (shopkeeper) about the, the traditional Kashmiri fire-pot. There is also a brief discussion on various types of fire-pots, namely, common, (a bride's fire-pot), and (children's fire-pot).

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is this?

exchanges.speaker: Shopkeeper
exchanges.english: It is called a.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Say that again (please).

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is it used for?

exchanges.speaker: Shopkeeper
exchanges.english: One puts live charcoal in the. In cold weather we use it to keep our bodies w.mp3.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Really? Does one w.mp3 one's body with the.

exchanges.speaker: Shopkeeper
exchanges.english: Yes, sir.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How does one do that?

exchanges.speaker: Shopkeeper
exchanges.english: The is put inside the phiran or in a blanket.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: The must cause burns.

exchanges.speaker: Shopkeeper
exchanges.english: Yes, sometimes the does cause burns.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is there only one type of?

exchanges.speaker: Shopkeeper
exchanges.english: No, sir, there are many types of fire-pots.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: (Please) show me two or three other types.

title: Going to Dal Lake
intro: A conversation with a na:vivo:l (a boatman) about sight-seeing on and around Dal Lake. Brief references are made to the gardens on the banks of Dal Lake such as Nishat, Shalimar, Chashma Shahi.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Hello there, boatman!

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: Yes, sir.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How far is Dal Lake from here?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: This is Habba Kadal. Amira Kadal is one mile from here. It will take an hour from Amira Kadal (Note: Kadal means a bridge).

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: We do not have much time. How long will it take to see Dal Lake?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: If we hurry, it will take us thre to four hours.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Can we return before it gets dark?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: Yes, certainly.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, what are the things worth seeing at Dal Lake?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: Dal Lake is very big. On the banks of Dal Lake there are three famous gardens.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is on Dal Lake?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: In Dal Lake, there are two islets: Golden Island and Silver Island.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What are the names of the gardens?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: The three famous gardens are Nishat, Shalimar, and Chashman Shahi.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is in the gardens?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: In the gardens there are flower beds, fountains, and beautiful balconies.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Are there flowers in Dal Lake?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: Yes, there are lotuses and vegetables in Dal Lake. There are aso many houseboats on Dal Lake.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Can we get something to eat there?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: Yes, there are many hotels there. One can also get a (Sama:va:r and Kashmiri Cups) there. Ashok: All right, let us go then.

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: (Please) come into the boat.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much do I have to pay?

exchanges.speaker: Boatman
exchanges.english: Only fifteen rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, let's go.

title: Visiting Wular Lake
intro: This concerns a conversation on visiting Wular Lake, the largest lake in India and the second largest lake in Asia. It is about thirty miles from Srinagar.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, Mohan. How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Greetings, I'm fine. How are you? Did you finish sight-seeing in Kashmir? Where are you planning to go now?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I am thinking of going to Wular lake. What do you say?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Before leaving Kashmir, you should definitely go to see Wular Lake.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, everybody says the same thing. How does one go there, and how long does it take?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: From Srinagar one goes by bus to Sopore, and from Sopore one goes by boat.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is there any other way?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, many people go by boat from Srinagar, or (they go) by bus up to Bandipora.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is Wular Lake famous for?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Wular Lake is the biggest lake in India. It is said that it is the second biggest lake in Asia.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How big is it?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: It is fourteen miles long. One cannot see one bank from the other. When it is windy, waves rise from all sides.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How many days does it take to visit Wular Lake?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: If you go by bus or car, it will take you just one day. Many people stay there for two or three days, living in a large houseboat near the bank.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, I do not have that much time. I have to go to Delhi.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Then you should go by bus.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, I will do that. I have to get ready. Goodbye.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Goodbye.

title: Going to Gulmarg
intro: This concerns a conversation on visiting Gulmarg, the available modes of transportation, and sights in and around Gulmarg. It is a favorite place with the tourists, and has the only skiing facilities in Kashmir.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, Mohanji.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, all right.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: How is your sightseeing in Kashmir going?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: It will take some more time.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Where are you going next?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I am thinking of going to Gulmarg for two or three days.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, why don't you go. Gulmarg is a very nice place. Do you know how one goes there?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: That is what I have to ask (you). How does one go there?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: It is very easy to go to Gulmarg. From Srinagar you take a bus up to Tangmarg.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What arrangements are there from Tangmarg?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: From Tangmarg it is three miles to Gulmarg. From there, you can make arrangements for a horse, or you can cover those three miles on foot.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is there at Gulmarg?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Gulmarg is avery attractive spot up in the mountains.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Are there trees and flowers at Gulmarg?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, Gulmarg is full of deodar trees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is there some arrangement for tourists to stay there?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, there are many hotels and tourist huts there. There is also a dak bungalow there.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I expect to stay there for two or three days.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: While there, you will be able to play golf and also go to Khilanmarg.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How far is Khilanmarg from Gulmarg?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: It should be three or four miles (from Gulmarg). You should go (there) on horseback.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right. I will definitely go to Gulmarg. Goodbye.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Goodbye.

title: Visiting Pahalgam
intro: This concerns a conversation on visiting, a tourist site about sixty miles from Srinagar. There is also a brief discussion of its surroundings and the facilities available at.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, Mohanji. How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Greetings, I am fine.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I came to ask what arrangements I have to make for going to Pahalgam.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: First, you return from Gulmarg to Srinagar. Afterwards, you should go to Pahalgam.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Which way do we (take to) go to Pahalgam?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: One goes to Pahalgam by an entirely different route.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is Pahalgam prettier than Gulmarg?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: That is a difficult question (to answer). Pahalgam has one kind of scenery, and Gulmarg has another.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the difference?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Pahalgam is at the foot of a mountain. There you have the beautiful Lidder and other streams, as well as forests.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Are there (any) hotels there?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Oh yes, many. Also, there is a tourist house there as well as a dak bungalow.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is it possible to go sight-seeing by a boat at Pahalgam?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: No, boating is not possible because the Lidder is a very fast moving and dangerous stream. It is difficult to row boats in it.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, what places are worth seeing near Pahalgam?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: On your way to Pahalgam, you will visit Anantnag, Achabal, Kokarnag, and Mattan.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Are three or four days enough (for the trip)?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, of course.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, p.mp3it me to leave. Goodbye.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Goodbye.

title: A Kashmiri shawl merchant
intro: This concerns a conversation with a traditional shawl merchant about buying Kashmiri shawls. There is also a brief discussion of raphal shawls, pashmina shawls, k'amkha:b shawls, shahtoos, and ring shawls.

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: Greetings, sir. What are you looking for?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I am looking for a Kashmiri shawl and a Kashmiri saree.

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: (Please) have a seat. I shall arrange for all the samples to be brought out.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What types of shawls do you have?

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: We have shawls of raphal and of pashmina.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: But pashmina must be very expensive?

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: Yes, pashmina is more expensive than raphal.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is there anything even better than pashmina?

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: Yes, the k'amkha:b, shahtoos, and ring shawls are more expensive than pashmina.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: These must be very expensive?

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: Yes, these are more expensive than pashmina.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is this shawl made of?

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: This shawl is made of pashmina.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: This shawl has very fine embroidery on it, but the color is not good. Do you have a red or a white shawl?

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: Do you like that red shawl over there?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I like its color, but the work on it is not good.

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: All right, we will show you another shawl, but its price is a little higher.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: If the shawl is good, the price does not matter.

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: See what embroidery and what color it has!

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, this has good embroidery and good color, too. What is the length of this (piece)?

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: This is four yards long.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is its width?

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: Two yards.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is this pashmina or raphal?

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: This is very fine pashmina.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How much does this shawl cost?

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: This shawl costs three hundred rupees.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, I will buy it.

exchanges.speaker: Shawl merchant
exchanges.english: Would you like to buy something else?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, not today.

title: Visiting Hazratbal
intro: A conversation on visiting the mosque at Hazratbal, ten miles from downtown Srinagar. The mosque, in which a sacred hair of the Prophet Mohammad is preserved, is situated on the western bank of Dal Lake. It was built by Shah Jehan, and is an excellent blend of Mughal and Kashmiri architecture.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Hello there, taxi driver!

exchanges.speaker: Cab driver
exchanges.english: Yes, sir.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: We want to go to Hazratbal. How far is it (from here)?

exchanges.speaker: Cab driver
exchanges.english: From here, Hazratbal is about five or six miles.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How long will it take?

exchanges.speaker: Cab driver
exchanges.english: (It) will take just fifteen or twenty minutes.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Where is Hazratbal located?

exchanges.speaker: Cab driver
exchanges.english: Hazratbal is close to Nigin Lake near Kashmir University.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is there at Hazratbal?

exchanges.speaker: Cab driver
exchanges.english: There is a famous mosque at Hazratbal where Muslims go each Friday to offer prayers. There is also the (sacred) hair of the Prophet Mohammad.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Is Hazratbal on the bank of Dal Lake?

exchanges.speaker: Cab driver
exchanges.english: No, Hazratbal is on the bank of Nigin Lake.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Can one go there only by taxi or tonga?

exchanges.speaker: Cab driver
exchanges.english: No, people go there by shikara, too.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How long does it take to go there by shikara?

exchanges.speaker: Cab driver
exchanges.english: From Amira Kadal, it takes two or three hours.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: No, we do not have that much time. We shall go by taxi.

exchanges.speaker: Cab driver
exchanges.english: Please, get in.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, let's go!

title: Visiting Tulmul
intro: A conversation on visiting the Hindu temple Tulmul located near Gandarbal about twenty miles from Srinagar. It is also called khi:rbhava:ni:.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Greetings, Ashok. Where have you been all these days?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, Mohan. I have been sight-seeing.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Well, what (places) did you see?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Just three or four places. I saw Hazratbal, Dal Lake, Gulmarg, and Pahalgam. I am (now) thinking of visiting Tulmul.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, you (must) do that.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, that's what I came to talk to you about. Tell me, how far is Tulmul from here?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Tulmul is about fifteen miles away, but it is not difficult to get there. From Amira Kadal both buses and tongas (horse carriages) go there. Many people go there in a large houseboat, but that takes a lot of time.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Can one return the same day, or does one stay there overnight?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, many people go there in the morning and return in the evening. But they also have provisions there for staying overnight.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Tell me, what is Tulmul famous for? Mohan: I will tell you. Tulmul is a famous shrine of the Hindus. There is an idol of the goddess bhava:ni: there, in the middle of a spring.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Do people worship this idol?

title: Visiting the Amarnath Cave
intro: Contextual Focus A conversation on visiting the Amarnath Cave. This cave is situated at a height of more than twelve thousand feet above sea level. Around the month of August, it attracts religious-minded Hindus from all over India (see Notes). Grammatical Focus Note the use of (see Notes). The classifier 'thousands' is introduced. The verbs 'to have to' and 'to enjoy' appear. Lexical Focus Note the following lexical set:

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Hello, Mohan. How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Hello. (Yes) I am fine. When did you return from Tulmul?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I came yesterday. This is the time (for me) to visit the Amarnath Cave. I am thinking of going there. How far is it?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Amarnath is about eighty miles from Srinagar. Go, you will enjoy it very much.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How does one go there?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: One goes by bus up to Pahalgam, and from Pahalgam you may go on horseback or on a stretcher.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How many people go there?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Oh, thousands. They come from all parts of India. A large number of sadhus also go there. The government makes arrangements for everything. Ashok: What kind of arrangements are there?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Everything, of course: doctors, medicine, food, clothing, and police.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is the road to Amarnath like?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Up to Pahalgam, the road is good. After that one has to climb over mountains.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: That means the journey is difficult?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, very difficult. One has to walk over snow.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: At what elevation is Amarnath located?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: About thirteen thousand feet.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is there on the mountain top?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Amarnath is a place for pilgrimage. There is a spring and a cave on the top of the mountain. Before entering the cave, all the pilgrims take a bath in the spring.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What is inside the cave? Mohan: It is said that on a lingam of (Lord) Shiva, made of snow, appears in the cave. All the pilgrims worship it. At that time a pair of pigeons also appears there.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: It must be a beautiful place. How many days does it take to get there?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: It takes about four to five days.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Perhaps I will go to Amarnath.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Oh yes, you should go. You will enjoy it.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, goodbye. I will come tomorrow to talk to you again.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, by all means. Goodbye.

title: Visiting the Shankracharya temple
intro: Contextual Focus This concerns a conversation on visiting (the Shankracharya Temple), situated on the top of a mountain to the south of Dal Lake. It is also called Takht-i-Sulaiman 'Solomon's Throne'. Grammatical Focus Note the use of (see Notes), and 'one by one'. The following verbs appear: 'to look someone up' and 'to be able to see'. Lexical Focus Note the following lexical set:

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Greetings, Mohan. How are you?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: (Yes) All right. What's new?

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I am thinking of visiting before leaving for Delhi.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, you should visit. It is not very far. It is only two or three miles from Amirakadal.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Yes, one can see it from here. Is the climb up the mountain (of ) difficult?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Oh, no. There is a nice path inthe front for going up to. When you come near the top, you will find many steps.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Can a car go up to the top?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, there is a road for cars and jeeps. There is a television station on top of.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: What else is there on top of?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: There is a temple at the top of. In the temple there is an idol of Lord Shiva. People go up there daily to offer their prayers.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: Do the people offer prayers one-by-one?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: No, in the morning people offer prayers jointly in the f.mp3 of an a:rti:.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: How high is it ()?

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: It is about one thousand feet high.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: I suppose one must get a beautiful view from the top of.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, one can see the whole of Srinagar from. You could also see Dal Lake and Hari Parbat.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: All right, then I shall climb tomorrow.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Yes, certainly you should climb it.

exchanges.speaker: Ashok
exchanges.english: (Please) allow me to leave now. Goodbye.

exchanges.speaker: Mohan
exchanges.english: Goodbye. Look me up before leaving for Delhi.

title: Come, O Gardener by Gulam Ahmad Mehjoor
The Song of a Boatman from Dal Lake by Dina Nath 'Nadim'
